pros: Simucube Button adjusts FFB live while racing
5-year replaceable battery via rear access window
Includes SQR at no extra cost
cons: No display, no rev LED strip
2.1 kg, heavier than most formula wheels
1.notes: First SC wireless wheel. Orange and Black editions are otherwise identical.

pros: 320mm round rim suited to rally, drift, and oval
Real leather grip allows controlled slide for counter-steer
Native SC2 wireless, no cable setup
Includes SQR and Simucube Button for live FFB adjust
cons: No display, no rev LEDs, no SimHub
Leather shows wear faster than alcantara
2.1 kg, heavier than ideal for some FFB setups
2.notes: 320mm round wheel with native SC wireless at a moderate price. Reasonable oval option on a budget.

pros: 20 total inputs, the highest count in the SC native lineup
18 programmable RGB LEDs via Simucube Tuner
Hall-effect dual clutch suited to iRacing bite-point starts
CNC aluminum with alcantara or leather options
Two 7-way switches, thumb encoders, and rotaries
Link Hub already owned, so LEDs are unlocked immediately
cons: LEDs work only via Simucube Tuner, not SimHub
$800+ is steep next to Cube Controls or SRB at similar input counts
3.notes: SC native wireless GT wheel. Owning the Link Hub means LED support is immediately available. Accessories: sold as Alcantara or Leather grip SKU; 18 RGB LEDs require Link Hub.

pros: Full die-cast metal frame, the most durable SC wheel currently made
12 RGB backlit buttons with interchangeable caps
Quiet paddles suited to long sessions
cons: Newer product with less community data
Fewer inputs than the Valo GT-23
Compact size may not suit larger hands
4.notes: 2025 release. Entry into the Savu line. Accessories: optional Savu Extra Paddle Kit adds dual clutch and extra paddles via rear Torx access.

pros: All Savu Sport features plus more encoders and 7-way switches
Top-mounted LED telemetry strip (RPM, flags, shift)
Metal frame with interchangeable button caps
Strong input ergonomics in the SC native lineup
cons: LED strip is Simucube Tuner only, not SimHub
Premium price for a screenless wheel
Small diameter not ideal for oval
5.notes: 2025 SC native wireless wheel. LED strip differentiates it from the Sport. Accessories: optional Savu Extra Paddle Kit (shared with Sport); interchangeable RGB button caps.

pros: Only SC-ecosystem wheel with full SimHub dashboard support
4-inch high-res display with 21 RGB shift LEDs
No battery concerns, always powered via USB cable
14 RGB automotive buttons and 4 thumb encoders
cons: USB cable required, no wireless option
300mm formula dimensions, not ideal for oval
6.notes: Display + SimHub option that stays in the SC ecosystem. Accessories: ships with SC Link QR; Ascher Gen6 magnetic paddle adjustable 260-800g via included spacers.

pros: LightBridge: no battery, no cable, no maintenance
60Hz 600MHz display, the fastest on any sim wheel
AdaptiveBeamTec LEDs show ABS/TC around the encoders
cons: Link QR adapter needed for SC2
7.notes: Technologically advanced wheel for SC2. The contactless connection is unique in sim racing. Accessories: front-plate options in Twill Carbon, Forged Carbon, or CNC satin black aluminium; removable decals.

pros: Closed rubber grips suited to gloveless or sweaty hands
Strong signal via external stub antenna
SC wireless: inputs read directly in SC2
cons: Only 12 inputs, the lowest in the Ascher lineup
No display, no LEDs, no SimHub
Older design vs the newer F64
8.notes: Suited to iRacing drivers who race gloveless and want a clean grip. Accessories: Gen6 magnetic paddle force adjustable via spacers; bead-blasted shifter set available.

pros: USB-C rechargeable, up to 800 hours per charge
Dual clutch with analog bite point
Rubberized ergonomic grips with thumb inserts
More inputs than the F28 (joysticks, rotary, encoders)
cons: No display, no RGB LEDs, no SimHub
Expensive for a screenless wireless wheel
9.notes: Ascher SC wireless wheel with the highest input count and analog clutch. Accessories: Gen6 silenced magnetic paddles; force-mod kit (260-800g) and label sheet sets included.

pros: Universal: works on any wheelbase with a 70mm hub
Same 24 inputs as the SC version, including dual analog clutch
cons: No display, no SimHub, no LEDs
10.notes: Choose USB if you share the wheel across multiple bases. Accessories: same Gen6 paddle and force kit as F64-SC; 50 button labels and 72 encoder labels included.

pros: Ergonomics inspired by the McLaren Artura GT car
SC wireless, native SC2 input handling
Ascher build with adjustable magnetic paddles
cons: No display, no LEDs, no SimHub
Fewer inputs than the Pro version
11.notes: For iRacing GT3/GTE drivers who want McLaren aesthetics with SC wireless. Accessories: shares Artura Pro/Ultimate paddle architecture; magnetic silenced double-paddle option.

pros: Highest input count in the Artura line
cons: No display, no LEDs, no SimHub
12.notes: Step up from Sport. Suited to iRacing GT drivers. Accessories: Gen6 magnetic and silenced double paddles plus motorsport clutch paddles standard.

13.notes: GT4 livery and design variant. Accessories: real GT4 paddle hardware; Ultimate-tier 14-button RGB plate sold as upgrade option.

pros: Brings SC wireless to any 70mm PCD motorsport rim
24 inputs, the practical maximum for a plate
Suited to oval when paired with a 320mm+ round rim
Works with Sparco P310, OMP 320, Momo Mod 78, MPI flat rim
cons: No display, no SimHub, no LEDs
14.notes: Pair with a flat motorsport round rim for oval and Simucube use. Accessories: ships with full label/sticker pack and adjustable magnetic paddle force kit (260-800g).

pros: Works with any 70mm PCD round rim
Less expensive than the SC wireless plate
cons: Cable management with a round rim can be tricky
15.notes: Budget option for oval if you don't need wireless. Accessories: 16-input plate ships with label sheets; paddle position and resistance adjustable via spacers.

pros: 1,150g with hub, light for the class
SimHub RGB buttons for visual telemetry
Bluetooth connects direct to PC, bypassing the wheelbase
ELOI shifter and clutch module, fast and adjustable
cons: Bluetooth wireless adds slight latency vs wired
Hub sold separately on the base version
16.notes: 2025 release targeting the $400 segment. SimHub RGB and Cube quality at entry pricing. Accessories: 3 SKUs, Base €399, Premium Black/Blue €449 (adds metal knobs and DD hub); ELOI paddle module.

pros: Full metal rotary encoders with strong tactile click at this price
Choice of R-Spec or GT shifters
17.notes: Step up from F-Core with metal encoders and better shifter choice. Accessories: GT (Red/Black) or Formula (Blue/Black) trim; 2 or 4 paddles; carbon-poly or full-aluminum shifters.

cons: Pricey vs the CSX-3, which adds a touchscreen for €231 more
18.notes: Formula specialist option for open-wheel iRacing. Accessories: F-Pro Color Edition variant; Universal Clutches add-on (full-aluminum, adjustable length).

pros: Full-color touchscreen display, unique in the Cube lineup
SimHub full dashboard support on screen
RGB buttons and full carbon body
19.notes: Cube's flagship formula. Touchscreen is useful for iRacing pit settings. Accessories: CSX-3 Color Edition; CSX-3 Extra Paddle CFRP kit converts to 6-paddle (4P or 6P SKUs).

pros: Three tiers: Sport, Cube, and Zero (max inputs)
20.notes: For iRacing GT, choose Zero for maximum inputs. Accessories: Black/Blue colorways with 2 or 4 paddle options; Universal Clutches add-on compatible.

pros: 320mm round rim in Italian suede or perforated leather
Full GT Pro V2 button box included
cons: More expensive than standalone round rim options
21.notes: Round wheel with full button system. Reasonable oval option. Accessories: sold in Round Suede or Perforated Leather grip SKUs (separate products).

pros: 5-inch touchscreen, the largest in the Cube lineup
22.notes: Cube's GT flagship. A choice if you want a display on a GT-style wheel. Accessories: front plate in 4 colors (black/white/green/red); interchangeable grip swaps 30→32cm diameter.

pros: Official Mercedes-AMG GT Track Series replica
Same wheel used on the real GT2/GTE car
Paddle shifters from the real AMG car
23.notes: Official Mercedes-AMG GT Track Series replica. Accessories: official AMG sticker sheet add-on; uses real-car shifter hardware.

pros: Custom PU overmolded rubber grip with strong durability
SC wireless: inputs read directly in True Drive
cons: No display, no SimHub, no LEDs
Ships from Slovenia, duties for US buyers
24.notes: GT3 sim wheel with a distinctive rubber grip. Accessories: SWW2 (newer wireless) and Special USB editions sold as separate SKUs.

pros: All GT3 V2 strengths with updated SWW2 wireless hardware
cons: Same input limitations as the V2
25.notes: Buy SWW2 over the original V2 if ordering new for the updated wireless hardware.

pros: Same PU rubber grip and build as the wireless version
26.notes: Suited to households running multiple wheelbases.

pros: Built on the genuine OMP Alu GT rim
Otto P9 buttons and Elma encoders
cons: EU shipping duties for US buyers
27.notes: For drivers who value FFB mass and authentic motorsport rim feel. Accessories: built on a real OMP rim; uses Otto P9 buttons and Elma encoders (no separate kits).

pros: 28 inputs, the maximum allowed on SC wireless
All 28 inputs read natively in True Drive
28.notes: If maximum SC wireless inputs is the goal, pair with a flat oval rim. Accessories: full billet aluminum 28-input plate; pair with any flat motorsport rim.

pros: More inputs than the GT3 V2
29.notes: Sits between BB Ultra and GT3 V2 in the SRB lineup. Accessories: 30cm overmolded PU rubber grip is the differentiator; no separate paddle kit.

pros: Most affordable GSI wheel and the entry into the ecosystem
SimHub native for buttons and LEDs
30.notes: GSI value pick. SimHub native suits iRacing telemetry users. Accessories: optional GSI AH50 hub upgrade for ergonomics (shared across the GSI lineup).

pros: FPE V2 build without display, so lighter overall
31.notes: Choose over the FPE V2 if you use an external dash and want lighter weight. Accessories: Panda/Phantom/Blackout/Prime/Custom colorways; new dust-resistant silicone grip.

pros: 66 individually addressable RGB LEDs via SimHub
6.5 Ncm encoders with strong tactile click
Universal QR works with most wheelbases via 70mm adapter
cons: Requires 70mm adapter for SQR (or use Simucube Edition for native fit)
32.notes: Standard FPE V2, the universal-mount formula flagship. For native SC2 mounting see id 159 (Simucube Edition). Accessories: 7 colorways (Phantom/Ascent/Blackout/Prime/Suzuki/TKX/Custom); 2- or 4-paddle, alu or carbon.

pros: Same 57 inputs, 66 RGB, LCD, and 6.5 Ncm encoders as the standard FPE V2
Integrated SQR hub mounts directly on Simucube 2, no adapter needed
Tightest mechanical fit on the SC2 ecosystem
cons: Integrated SQR limits use on non-Simucube bases (vs the universal-mount standard FPE V2 at id 32)
$179 premium over the standard for the SQR integration
159.notes: FPE V2 with an integrated SQR hub that bolts directly to Simucube 2 with no adapter. Pick this over id 32 if you race exclusively on SC2.

pros: Step above the FPE V2 in ergonomics
33.notes: iRacing esports-level option. The FPE V2 covers most of the same ground for less. Accessories: Ascent/Prime/Phantom/Suzuki/TKX/Custom colorways; shares FPE V2 rotaries.

pros: 4.3-inch LCD touchscreen and RPM LED bar
Used by IndyCar driver Tony Kanaan
34.notes: Top-tier GSI option. Accessories: 4- or 6-paddle SKUs (alu or carbon); optional dual-clutch and top paddles add-on; AH50 hub.

pros: One-piece carbon construction (rim and face are a single unit)
320mm round shape suited to GT, oval, and drift
Silicone overmolded handles for strong grip
35.notes: All-in-one oval/GT round wheel with display and buttons. Accessories: Scarlet/Sable/Phantom/Prime colorways; one-piece molded carbon construction.

pros: Dedicated 320mm oval rim designed for stock car racing
LED Matrix add-on for flags, gear, and alerts
Future Powerlink QR passthrough wireless planned
36.notes: iRacing oval option. The only system with an officially labeled 'Oval' rim SKU. Accessories: choose GT 320/300mm or Oval 320mm rim; 2 or 4 paddles in alu or carbon; interchangeable grips.

37.notes: Value oval iRacing pick. Purpose-built oval rim with full electronics. Accessories: rim swappable to GT 320/300mm; Blackout colorway available; 2/4-paddle alu/carbon options.

pros: SimHub native for all LEDs and inputs
NextGen QuickShift: short-stroke modular carbon paddles
cons: No display in the PULSE version
38.notes: Strong thumb ergonomics. PULSE is the 2026 successor to the Delta Core, sharing the chassis with refreshed electronics. Accessories: 6 carbon paddles standard; removable decal/sticker kit available.

pros: AdaptiveBeamTec shows ABS/TC visually around encoders
Interchangeable front covers (CF, forged CF, satin Al)
39.notes: Strong ergonomic engineering. The Delta Pro SC is the wireless/no-battery version. Accessories: front in Twill Carbon, Forged Carbon, or CNC Satin Black Alu; removable QR-latched decal kit.

pros: Strong feature set at $399 with display included
12-layer 3D CF front (same construction as Le Mans race cars)
Includes 50mm and 70mm adapters, fits SQR directly
cons: Display limited to preset dash pages
Some early reports of encoder skipping
40.notes: Few wheels at $399 include a display alongside this build quality. Accessories: MagFlip rocker shifter with 3 force settings (550/660/900gf); Hall clutch with adjustable bite.

pros: 3D ergonomic design, not flat like most formula wheels
VR-friendly: 3D layout supports finding controls without looking
cons: Conspit Link less mature than rivals
41.notes: Distinctive 3D ergonomic formula layout. Suited to GT and prototype racing in iRacing. Accessories: dual-clutch and rocker MagFlip paddles; Enduro grip standard for endurance racing.

pros: Vertical portrait touchscreen, the first of its kind on a sim wheel
Analog clutch paddles for bite-point starts
cons: Rubber grips store heat, gloves recommended
Bottom thumb encoders sit too low
No in-house hub, SQR sold separately
42.notes: French design with a vertical screen that takes some adaptation. Accessories: optional forged carbon front plate, custom front sticker, and colored grip kits.

pros: P1Sim 2026 flagship and current production model
F1-style with full analog clutch system
Forged carbon fiber faceplate with CNC aluminum
43.notes: Current 2026 P1Sim formula flagship. Replaced the older Eau Rouge / EVO / Plus lineup (see id 160, 161 for those discontinued predecessors). Accessories: 4-to-6 paddle configurable; forged carbon front plate and custom plate stickers available.

pros: Hall sensor shifters, more precise than the original Eau Rouge
New joystick for better thumb ergonomics
More affordable than the Plus when available
cons: No longer sold by P1Sim, folded into the Eau Rouge APEX (id 43)
Same rubber grip heat issues as the Apex
160.notes: P1Sim folded the EVO and Plus into the Apex flagship in 2026. Listed for reference and used-market searches. Buy URL points to the Apex since the EVO product page no longer exists.

pros: Highest-spec P1Sim wheel before the Apex consolidation
Forged or twill carbon fiber option
All EVO improvements plus added control refinements
cons: No longer sold by P1Sim, folded into the Eau Rouge APEX (id 43)
Last new units sold below MSRP
161.notes: P1Sim folded the Plus into the Apex flagship in 2026. Listed for reference and used-market searches. Buy URL redirects to the Apex since the Plus product page no longer exists.

pros: Elma encoders, each with 5 addressable RGB LEDs
On-wheel bite-point calibration, no software needed
EVO2-SC is the SC wireless variant, pairs natively with SC2 / Link Hub
cons: Smaller brand with less community data
46.notes: Strong SimHub integration. Accessories: EVO:NS dual-clutch and EVO:XN sister SKUs; optional dual-clutch carbon paddle upgrade.

pros: Genuine Sparco rim made in Italy
cons: 50.8mm PCD only, hub adapter needed for SQR
Low input count for the price (12 inputs)
47.notes: Authentic motorsport pedigree with a genuine Sparco rim. Accessories: optional Quick Adjust paddle upgrade; magnetic carbon-fiber paddles standard.

pros: 9 rotary dials, the most of any sim wheel
48.notes: Bespoke motorsport-grade option for setups with unlimited budget. Accessories: shares the PSE magnetic paddle ecosystem; Quick Adjust paddle option available.

49.notes: For WEC/IMSA simulation use. Accessories: shares the PSE magnetic carbon paddle hardware; Quick Adjust upgrade available across the PSE range.

pros: Exact replica of the Porsche 911 RSR 991.2
cons: Sold out as of April 2026, check back periodically
50.notes: Collector-tier replica. Suited to 911 RSR fans in iRacing/LMU. Accessories: 16.7M-color RGB per-button customization; 6061 aluminum shifters, clutches, and paddles all replaceable.

pros: 1:1 replica using Mercedes-AMG F1 CAD data
Same encoders as the real Hamilton/Russell car
14 buttons and 9 rotary switches (matches the real F1)
cons: $2,700, the most expensive readily available sim wheel
51.notes: Authentic F1 sim option. Suited to iRacing formula specialists with a serious budget. Accessories: officially licensed; per-button RGB customization (no separate paddle/grip SKUs sold).

pros: Value-priced at $399 with aluminum CNC buttons
Pro driver co-designed with deep iRacing connections
Single PCB design for reliable, simple electronics
Universal 70mm PCD mounts directly to SQR
cons: No display, no SimHub, no LEDs
Minimalist by philosophy, no extra features
52.notes: For iRacing racers who want zero distractions. Accessories: fully replaceable silicone grips (3 screws); VRS Quick Release add-on (80mm or 160mm shaft).

pros: SC wireless plus a real motorsport round rim of choice
Otto P9 buttons and NKK toggles, race-car switch quality
Gen7 CNC magnetic paddles with carbon paddles
Pairs with 320mm+ round rims for oval use
cons: No display, no SimHub (SC wireless limit)
53.notes: Common path to run a Sparco P310 or OMP round rim with SC wireless. Suited to oval and rally setups.

pros: 28 inputs, the maximum on the SC wireless system
Otto P9 buttons, NKK toggles, CTS encoders
cons: No off-the-shelf product page, contact SimCore for a custom build
54.notes: Maximum SC wireless inputs on a real OMP rim. Accessories: SimCore also sells RD22-WS (rally/drift) and STD20 plate variants for other rim shapes.

pros: Laser-scan replica of the real 720s GT3 rim
Choice of Alcantara or synthetic leather grip
cons: Limited inputs (14) vs dedicated formula wheels
Order configs can take time to spec
55.notes: 720s GT3 livery on the standard SimLine replica electronics platform. One of the more popular SimLine picks alongside the AMG GT3. Accessories: Alcantara or synthetic leather grip SKU; optional NRG quick release.

pros: Laser-scan replica of the real Huracan GT3 rim
Choice of Alcantara or synthetic leather grip
cons: Limited inputs (14) vs dedicated formula wheels
Order configs can take time to spec
162.notes: Huracan GT3 livery on the standard SimLine replica electronics platform. Accessories: Alcantara or synthetic leather grip SKU; optional NRG quick release.

pros: Laser-scan replica of the real AMG GT3 rim
Choice of Alcantara or synthetic leather grip
cons: Limited inputs (14) vs dedicated formula wheels
Order configs can take time to spec
163.notes: AMG GT3 livery on the standard SimLine replica electronics platform. Among the more popular SimLine SKUs. Accessories: Alcantara or synthetic leather grip SKU; optional NRG quick release.

pros: Laser-scan replica of the real Corvette C7R rim
Choice of Alcantara or synthetic leather grip
cons: Limited inputs (14) vs dedicated formula wheels
Order configs can take time to spec
164.notes: Corvette C7R livery on the standard SimLine replica electronics platform. Accessories: Alcantara or synthetic leather grip SKU; optional NRG quick release.

pros: Laser-scan replica of the real Fiesta WRC rim
Choice of Alcantara or synthetic leather grip
cons: Limited inputs (14) vs dedicated formula wheels
Order configs can take time to spec
165.notes: Fiesta WRC livery on the standard SimLine replica electronics platform. Rally-spec replica. Accessories: Alcantara or synthetic leather grip SKU; optional NRG quick release.

pros: Laser-scan replica of the real R5 WRC rim
Choice of Alcantara or synthetic leather grip
cons: Limited inputs (14) vs dedicated formula wheels
Order configs can take time to spec
166.notes: R5 WRC livery on the standard SimLine replica electronics platform. Rally R5-class replica. Accessories: Alcantara or synthetic leather grip SKU; optional NRG quick release.

pros: Laser-scan replica of the real GT3-R rim
Choice of Alcantara or synthetic leather grip
cons: Limited inputs (14) vs dedicated formula wheels
Order configs can take time to spec
167.notes: GT3-R livery on the standard SimLine replica electronics platform. Accessories: Alcantara or synthetic leather grip SKU; optional NRG quick release.

pros: Laser-scan replica of the real GT3 Cup rim
Choice of Alcantara or synthetic leather grip
cons: Limited inputs (14) vs dedicated formula wheels
Order configs can take time to spec
168.notes: GT3 Cup livery on the standard SimLine replica electronics platform. Porsche Cup-style replica. Accessories: Alcantara or synthetic leather grip SKU; optional NRG quick release.

pros: Laser-scan replica of the real WRC/TCR rim
Choice of Alcantara or synthetic leather grip
cons: Limited inputs (14) vs dedicated formula wheels
Order configs can take time to spec
169.notes: WRC/TCR livery on the standard SimLine replica electronics platform with the largest SimLine rim. Accessories: Alcantara or synthetic leather grip SKU; optional NRG quick release.

pros: SC wireless on a genuine MPI GT-RSC-310 round rim
cons: MPI rim is 310mm, just under the 315mm target
56.notes: Custom-build option suited to Australian and NZ buyers. Accessories: built to order with rim choice (MPI GT-RSC-310 or Sparco P310); 6-week build time.

pros: 25 RGB LEDs for shift and telemetry
cons: Asetek QR adapter needed for SC2 (~$50-70)
59.notes: Strong value for a display formula wheel after factoring in the adapter. Accessories: optional 6-paddle kit (matches Invicta); LMP/Suede/XL handle SKUs; Orange Button Color Kit.

60.notes: Strong USB price-to-feature ratio. Accessories: same MagFlip rocker shifter (550/660/900gf) and Hall clutch architecture as the SC2 SKU.

61.notes: Distinctive 3D ergonomic formula layout. Accessories: dual-clutch and MagFlip rocker; Enduro grip; modular RGB ergonomic buttons.

62.notes: All-in-one USB round wheel for oval/GT. Accessories: Scarlet/Sable/Phantom/Prime colorways; carbon one-piece construction; AH50 hub option.

63.notes: USB modular oval rim option. Accessories: rim swappable GT 320/300/Oval 320mm; 2/4-paddle alu/carbon; interchangeable grip system.

64.notes: Common Ascher choice for multi-base households. Accessories: same Gen6 magnetic paddles as the SC version; force adjustable 260-800g via included spacers.

65.notes: Budget oval plate. Pair with the MPI SimMax-D flat oval rim. Accessories: position-adjustable paddles; spacer/magnet kit for resistance tuning included.

pros: On-wheel bite-point calibration, no software needed
EVO2-USB is the universal USB variant, works with any wheelbase
66.notes: Strong SimHub integration. Accessories: forged carbon paddle upgrade; EVO:NS dual-clutch SKU and EVO:XN variant available.

67.notes: Display formula USB wheel with strong ergonomics. Accessories: 3 front-plate finishes (Twill/Forged carbon, CNC alu); 6 carbon paddles; quick-release decal kit.

69.notes: Arnage is P1Sim's signature model, distinct from the Eau Rouge family (consolidated into the Eau Rouge Apex flagship in 2026, see id 43). Accessories: forged carbon plate, custom stickers, colored grips, 4-to-6 paddle config across the range.

cons: No display, no SimHub, no LEDs (by design)
70.notes: Budget USB formula wheel for pure driving focus. Accessories: replaceable silicone grips; VRS Quick Release add-on with 80mm or 160mm shaft option.

pros: GSI specifically labels this the 'Oval' rim
cons: Moderately expensive with the Interlock base
71.notes: iRacing oval option. The only system in sim racing with an explicitly labeled 'Oval' rim SKU. Accessories: rim hot-swappable (GT 320/300/Oval 320mm); 2/4-paddle alu/carbon; interchangeable grip kit.

pros: 3 slotted ergonomic spokes for sprint car oval grip
6x70mm and 3x51mm PCD bolts to SQR directly
Real competition wheel for sprint, midget, and late model
cons: No buttons or electronics, pair with B24M-SC or B16M-USB plate
Total cost: $229 rim plus $634-$691 button plate
Sprint car shape (3 spokes) different from a traditional NASCAR round
Very large 381mm may feel bigger than a typical sim oval wheel
72.notes: Flattest, largest round rim available. If zero dish is required, this is the option. Pair with the Ascher B24M-SC for SC wireless. Accessories: also available as 14" or 15" NASCAR OE-14/15-RG; suede or RG grip wrap; Dual Speed Pods button add-ons.

pros: Native SC wireless, no cable setup
Includes SQR and Simucube Button for FFB adjust
Round option that stays in the SC ecosystem with no wires
cons: Only 13 inputs, limited for an oval car setup
73.notes: Wireless option for oval setups. Leather grip handles big oval slides well.

pros: Full GT Pro V2 button box included
74.notes: Round oval wheel with SimHub. Accessories: choose Round Suede or Perforated Leather grip SKU; 2 or 4 paddle variants.

pros: 40 inputs, the most of any round wheel here
75.notes: 320mm carbon round with display and buttons in a narrow market segment. Accessories: 4 colorways (Scarlet/Sable/Phantom/Prime); AH50 hub option for direct drive bases.

pros: One-piece CNC aluminum, 5mm carbon, and leather
9 RGB rev lights and 12 LED encoders
Modular NEO X Hub swaps to GT, Touring, or Wide rims
cons: USB cable for data unless on a Simagic base
Verify dish on the round variant
76.notes: Strong Simagic oval candidate. The previously-listed 'XGT Pro Round 320mm' was a misnomer; the actual SKU is the NEO X-330R Classic. Accessories: hub accepts XGT, XGT Pro, XGT Pro Round, XGTR rims; modular magnetic paddle modules.

pros: Real race-car rim quality and grip
SC wireless via the SimCore STD-WS GEN2 plate
Or use Ascher B16-USB for a wired budget setup
cons: No display, no SimHub (SC wireless plate)
OMP rim needs cleaning over time
77.notes: For oval drivers wanting authentic motorsport rim feel at a reasonable total cost. Accessories: SimCore plate available in 7 colorways; pair with any OMP/Sparco motorsport rim.

pros: Momo Mod 30 is the current 320mm successor to the Mod 78, near-flat dish
Pair with the SimCore STD-WS GEN2 SC wireless plate
cons: The Mod 78 was discontinued; the Mod 30 is the closest current match
No display, no SimHub (SC wireless plate)
78.notes: Classic motorsport look. The Mod 78 was discontinued; Ricmotech currently carries Mod 27/29/30/31, with the Mod 30 closest to the 320mm spec. Accessories: SimCore button plate sold in 7 colors; verify Momo Mod 78 dish flatness before ordering.

pros: Real Cup Series wheel used by NASCAR drivers
Dual Speed Pods available for buttons
cons: 3-3.25 inch deep dish, exceeds the <0.5 inch requirement
Listed for reference only per the stated spec
No buttons, needs Speed Pods or a separate plate
79.notes: Excluded per the dish rule. Many serious iRacing oval specialists use this exact wheel; the deep dish brings the wheel closer for arms-bent comfort over 30+ lap stints. Worth reconsidering the constraint. Accessories: optional Dual Speed Pods (drill-on button boxes); RG suede or all-suede wrap options.

pros: MOZA Pit House app for LED and FFB customization
Universal 70mm PCD via MOZA Universal Hub Kit
cons: MOZA Universal Hub Kit required for SC2 (~$50-100 extra)
No SimHub support, MOZA Pit House software only
80.notes: Affordable feature-rich DD wheel. Add the $50 Universal Hub Kit and it bolts to SQR. Accessories: MOZA Paddles Add-on Kit upgrades to 6 paddles; pairs with the Universal Hub Kit for SC bases.

pros: 310mm GT round rim, the closest to oval spec in the MOZA lineup
HD LCD display with quad-core processor
cons: MOZA Universal Hub Kit required for SC2 (~$50-100)
No SimHub, MOZA Pit House only
Hub kit mounting on SC2 requires a 70mm adapter workaround
Display setup less mature than SimHub
81.notes: 310mm round GT with display. Solid value if MOZA Pit House is acceptable instead of SimHub. Accessories: MOZA Paddles Add-on Kit (6-paddle upgrade); HD quad-core display; Universal Hub Kit option.

pros: 4.3-inch 800x480 touchscreen, swipe to change dash mid-race
Fully customizable dashboard in MOZA Pit House
10 RGB RPM LEDs and 6 telemetry LEDs
cons: MOZA Universal Hub Kit required for SC2 (~$50-100)
No SimHub support, Pit House only
Dashboard editor has a learning curve
82.notes: Display formula wheel at $649. Pit House supports custom dashboards. Competes with P1Sim and the Conspit 290GP. Accessories: 6 paddle shifters standard; MOZA Paddles Add-on Kit; aluminum QR; Universal Hub Kit option.

pros: 300mm GT round with magnetic dual-clutch paddles
MOZA dashboard sharing platform with importable community designs
Dual-clutch Hall sensor paddles plus forged carbon
cons: MOZA Universal Hub Kit required for SC2 (~$50-100)
No SimHub support, confirmed in reviews
$749 plus the hub kit reaches ~$800+ without SimHub
Mounting on SC2 requires a specific 70mm PCD workaround
83.notes: Circular display is unusual for sim racing. Without SimHub support, look at the GS V2P or FSR2 if that is required. Accessories: MOZA Paddles Add-on Kit (6-paddle upgrade); Universal Hub Kit for non-MOZA bases.

pros: 2.99-inch HD display with MOZA Pit House dashboards at $329
10 RGB buttons, 6 flag LEDs, and 12 RPM LEDs
5 rotary encoders, 2 seven-way funky switches, and 2 thumb encoders
Forged carbon paddles with Hall sensors and dual clutch
Universal Hub Kit brings it to SC2
cons: MOZA Universal Hub Kit required for SC2 (~$50-100)
No SimHub, MOZA Pit House only
300mm butterfly only, not ideal for oval
Some reports of 3-month wait for in-stock deliveries
84.notes: Released Jan 2026. Display wheel with dual clutch at $329 is strong value. Accessories: forged carbon dual-clutch paddle module; programmable as dual/single clutch or buttons.

pros: 325mm round rim meets oval spec at $329
10 RGB rev LEDs and 6 flag LEDs
Forged carbon paddles, dual clutch, and expandable to 6 paddles
6x70mm bolt pattern: rim is swappable for other 325mm rims
Universal Hub Kit brings it to SC2
cons: MOZA Universal Hub Kit required for SC2 (~$50-100)
No SimHub, MOZA Pit House only
325mm aluminum rim, not a leather-wrapped motorsport rim feel
Hub kit SC2 mounting requires a 70mm adapter
85.notes: Released Jan 2026. 325mm round display wheel at $329 is strong value. Swappable rim system is a bonus. Accessories: optional 6-paddle upgrade kit (DRS/KERS mappable); microfiber leather grip.

pros: Official Porsche replica using Mission R CAD data
5.4-inch flexible OLED, the first in sim racing (720p 60Hz curved)
Real-time telemetry: speed, lap, tire pressure, ABS, TC
CNC aluminum frame plus dual-clutch Hall sensor paddles
12 configurable controls and floating button design
cons: MOZA Universal Hub Kit required for SC2 (~$50-100)
No SimHub, MOZA Pit House only
$1,299 competes with the GRID 911 RSR ($2,247) and SC x Ascher ($1,499)

86.notes: First sim racing wheel with a flexible OLED display. 320mm size, official Porsche replica. Accessories: officially licensed Porsche replica; MOZA Paddles Add-on Kit compatible.

pros: MOZA Pit House display with telemetry
cons: MOZA Universal Hub Kit required for SC2 (~$50-100)
No SimHub, MOZA Pit House only
$1,299 for a Pit House-only display is steep
87.notes: Official Lamborghini SCV12 replica. Suited to GT3/LMDh fans racing the Huracan or Essenza. Accessories: MOZA Paddles Add-on Kit; Universal Hub Kit for third-party DD bases.

pros: Asetek ecosystem native for Asetek bases
Modular grips system, swap grip styles
cons: Asetek QR adapter needed for SC2 (~$50-70)
Grips sold separately on some configs
88.notes: Entry Asetek wheel. Strong value with SimHub support. Accessories: La Prima→Forte upgrade kit; LMP / Suede / Formula XL handle SKUs; Color Kit (button caps).

pros: 144 total input options, the largest configurable count here
4.3-inch 60Hz display with native support for both SimHub and RaceHub
Fully modular: swap handles (Formula, GT, LMP) without tools
CNC aluminum chassis, rigid and lightweight
Asetek QR included but removable, also works with 70mm SQR
Hand-signed by CEO and assembly tech
Swappable button color kits, grip styles, and encoder tops
cons: Asetek QR adapter needed for SC2 or use 70mm directly

89.notes: Modular formula wheel. SimHub display support is the differentiator vs the Asetek Forte. Accessories: 6-paddle factory; rotaries/switches swappable from La Prima/Forte; LMP/Suede/XL handles; Color Kits.

pros: Full carbon fiber body, handmade in EU and individually crafted
5-inch 854x480 touchscreen, the largest touch display in class
Plus 3 OLED encoder displays, 4 screens total on one wheel
Elma thumb encoders with tactile click
Official Alpine F1 Team simulator supplier
cons: EU-centric, shipping plus duties for US buyers
Each wheel handmade, slight variation expected
90.notes: Multi-screen display: 5-inch screen plus 3 OLED encoder readouts. Alpine F1 supplier. Accessories: 4- or 6-paddle SKUs; optional 3rd cam set (push/pull/latching, 2-20 presses programmable).

pros: Full carbon fiber body handmade in EU
4.3-inch USBD480 LCD display, SimHub and Simdash compatible
17 RGB LEDs (11 RPM + 6 flag), all configurable
91.notes: More affordable Rexing entry. 4.3-inch SimHub display at competitive pricing. Accessories: optional 3rd-paddle add-on; Hall clutch with 6 saved bite-point presets onboard.

pros: Full anodized CNC aluminum, solid build for the price
4 rotary encoders, 2 thumb encoders, and 2 five-way joysticks
GX12-4 to USB coiled cable for universal compatibility
Ships from Montreal with US/Canada free shipping and duties included
cons: SimHub buttons only, no full display support
Less brand recognition vs established names
~30mm dish (GT style), not flat for oval use
92.notes: Strong CNC aluminum GT wheel per dollar with SimHub LED support. Budget alternative to the Cube Controls F-Core EVO. Accessories: silicone ergo grip; adjustable-travel shift paddles; analog paddles configurable as dual clutch.

pros: SimHub full compatibility, all RGB lighting configurable via SimHub
Thumb encoders with dual-action 8 inputs each

Stiff and lightweight, low fatigue over long stints
Strong community reviews for SimHub integration
cons: EU-centric, import duties for US buyers
Smaller brand vs established names, less community data
93.notes: Dutch formula wheel with strong SimHub support. Accessories: 4 launch colorways; anodized knob color kits sold separately; extra magnets for shifter force.

pros: From the makers of high-end sim pedals, their first wheel
Ultra lightweight at 1,024g including battery, paddles, and clutches
Push and pull magnetic shifters work both directions (rally-style)
Dual clutch paddles plus 9 configurable RGB SimHub shift lights
60+ hr battery (600+ hr in low-power VR mode)
Wireless via own USB dongle, no ecosystem lock-in, works on any base
SimHub fully compatible for LEDs and telemetry
Independent wireless: not SC wireless, not Bluetooth, own receiver
cons: Proprietary wireless dongle adds a USB receiver to manage
€599 is competitive but display wheels exist at similar price
Cable-free wireless not available (needs USB dongle in PC)
94.notes: Highly ergonomic wheel designed for driver performance. Push/pull paddles and palm-to-fingertip grip are unique. Accessories: 24 button covers swappable across 14 positions; optional 70mm-pattern hub adapter (15mm depth).

pros: Display, dual clutch, and carbon paddles at $329
2.99-inch HD display with MOZA Pit House dashboards

pros: Fully modular: swap handles, grips, buttons

pros: 5-inch touchscreen plus 3 OLED encoder displays, 4 screens total

pros: Own wireless dongle, works on any base

pros: 13-inch true-to-life GT round rim, the closest MOZA size to oval spec
